Welcome back to the ultimate NoBS referral machine. We're in session number three here. And in this session, Dan will reveal three key elements you need as part of your NoBS referral machine as the actual part of the machine, the client newsletter. We're also going to talk about a powerful stealth way to get clients to refer even when they don't want to refer. So, let's start though with testimonials. And why are testimonies important? Are they still important today? Well, it's specific to referral activity. They're important because I feel better referring people to you if there's a lot of social proof. Right. It's not just me referring. Well, exactly. Right. Yeah. So, in general, they are still the most believable kind of proof. So, factual proof, scientific proof, even demonstration proof. You know, in infomercials, we always said that the easiest show to do is the show where the product is the star. So, you can shove a whole ham and six vegetables and five bananas in the top of the blender and make something. Right. However, even those, the public has a skepticism about the demonstration. Right. Because you could rig the demonstration. And by the way, you can rig that demonstration. But real people telling their stories, it's the most believable kind of proof there is. It can be the most motivational kind of proof because I want to be like this person and the result they got. I identify with this person. Right. And it can be the most interesting because human interest stories are inherently more interesting than facts, figures, science, studies, et cetera. So, you know, when you said are they still important today, that suggests a premise that, you know, this has worn out its welcome in the marketplace and nothing could be, you know, further from the truth. Now, specific to referrals, again, the more social proof you have and that you are visibly using, that I know the person I refer is going to encounter, then the less anxious I am about referring. It takes really the onus off of me at that point. Because now the person I've referred sees all of these other people and hears from all of these other people and is now really making a composite decision rather than a decision for which I have all the responsibility. Right. Yeah, exactly. So, we know that they're important. What's the best way to collect them? How do I go about it? I'm shocked that most business owners don't have any. Point of a gun. Right. Look, a lot of business owners don't have them. Right. A lot of business owners have them that have accumulated accidentally and they've been using them. But hardly anybody really is prioritizing the collection of them on a day by day, week by week, customer by customer, patient by patient basis, which is what should be done. So, one good way to collect them is to ask for them. Right. And if you are unwilling to ask, then it makes it all the more important to have very visible use. So, the use itself is asking. Sure. Right. So, this is what we do here. Yeah, exactly. So, if I come into your office and as we talked about before, the walls are covered with fake monnaies, that doesn't communicate anything to me except maybe you have good taste in art. Right. If the walls are covered with photographs and testimonials, big blown up and frame, all the way down the hallway, I'm starting to get the message. Right. Right. And so, the visible non-ask is important under any conditions, but it's even more important if you are absolutely going to refuse to sit down face to face with a customer, client, or patient and outright ask. Whatever you do, you have to have a system for this. So, if I am going to give you a testimonial, the worst thing you do is, hey, Dave, the next time you come in, if you write out and bring a testimony, well, you don't know how to do that. Right. And you don't know where you got a pen and you don't have a pad and you don't, I mean, on and on and on and on and on. I'm asking, you know, I'm asking, I'm asking my dog to take a picture of himself and, you know, email me a selfie, you know, I mean, anybody water would accommodate me, but he doesn't have fingers. I mean, it's a, where do you, where do you come up with that? I mean, where does that actually come back? I have no idea. I have no idea. I have no idea. I have no idea. I have no idea. I have no idea. I'm going to take a picture of himself and email him. It's in the, it's in the analogy, you know, compartment back here. An analogy archive. But I don't know what produces which one. And, and see, I heard, I heard too internally that you're inappropriate for use before I settled on the third one. So, so you need a system for people to provide testimonials. So some people will give a recorded message line that people can call in and record them. Some people will give them a sample book with here's 20 and now here's the form to fill out. Some will do it online. You know, if you ask somebody to go post a review for you, well, same dog, selfie, phone, there's got to be a process for this. There should be a reward for it, not necessarily bribe stated in advance and not necessarily a monetary kind, but at least a thank you note, at least a, you know, at least, hey, this is great. And I'm going to use it and you will see it, you know, I just the other day, I got one day before yesterday, I think, came in last week's Whiteback, which I'm taking care of this week and unsolicited, a really nice, you know, so quick photocopy of the first page and a green ink, thanks. This is terrific. Congratulations. And you're going to see it in either the August or the September news newsletter. Right. Well, better that than just wait and let them see it in a newsletter. I mean, he took the time to do so. You were a reward, right? And ultimately, in a brick and mortar business or a professional practice, if you're really serious about this, you will measure and manage it so that you are progressively rewarding those who are doing it a lot. And at some point, you are having a conversation with those who are not referring at all. You know, the other strategy is I've got chocolate here in my hand from the cookie. The other strategy is to write it yourself. So I actually just did this for my fiance who's a real estate agent and I'm doing the marketing for her obviously. And so she sold some houses and I wanted specific types of testimonials. And by the way, in the manual that you get with the system that you got with the system, it has a section in here on the different types of testimonials that you want. People don't get that. Let's talk a little bit about that in a second. But you want different types of testimonials. And so I wrote them and she got her clients to approve them and they literally approved them all word for word. And it was the easiest thing on the planet. So, you know, you need to go through it. If you know the customer's story, then you can either coach or you can write. And I've done it and feel free to change this if you need to. And they don't change it to me. The, you know, when I write copy for clients, I will often write the testimonial that should be here in the copy. Now you got to go find one that is real, that matches as closely to this as possible. Right. So what are the elements that make up a good testimonial? A great testimonial is, first of all, always some version of before and after. So it is a before and after picture textualized. So it is some version of I was fat, dumb, broke, miserable, unhappy, living in a cardboard box, disdained by everybody. And now I'm, you know, I always quote Richard Pryor's great joke about that was that his before and after is better than anybody else's is I was a poor black guy and I'm a rich white guy. That's as big a before and after as you can possibly have. But they all have a before and after element to them, drama helps. So if it's dramatic, right, if somebody only lost 35 pounds on the diet, but there was drama to it, I've been on 50 diets before, I was contemplating suicide, I was worried my husband was if there's drama to it, then the 35 pounds is the same as if she lost 350 pounds. So drama, details. So a lot of people leave out all the details, you know, and details matter to people. So there's the details of the person, first of all. So the worst is initials JL, right, because that could be in saturn made it up. And the best is all the identification that might matter to the people who are seeing it. So that might be that their parents of three kids aged four, seven and 14. It might be that their occupation might matter. It might mean that they're a military veteran might matter. So the details about the person, and then the details of the story, unless you are 146 character limited, you don't want to leave out the that it started on a dark and stormy night. You want those details. You want often you want your testimonials to erase specific objections. So you should know what the five or six reasons are that stop people from buying your thing, right, and your testimonials can erase those five or six objections. And then last, I would say that the best testimonials now are captured in and delivered in multimedia. So there's a place to use them in print. There's a place to use them spoken. There's a place to show them on video. Okay, so let's let's go over some you brought some testimonials that you felt were good testimonials. And again, we've got more stuff that I'm going to show you. I don't have them. You don't have the good testimonial down here where Darcy put them down here. Yes. Look at I got him now. She's Johnny on the spot. Yep. Look at that. Oh, she just right now being the being the wise asset. She just gave the thumbs up. Ah, there you go. Okay. Did I only have I got four. Fantastic. So I got to treat her and my daughter to a Savannah view suite at Animal Kingdom Lodge, the Cirque show, etc. My mom said it was her bestification ever. Because this is all facts and figures and money. And it's almost a B2B case history. Here's what we did. And here's what the results are. Then you have what I call pictorial testimonials. You don't even need to read the testimonial. Right. I have one, but you don't need to read it to get the point. She's made a picture of herself standing next to her airplane with thanks, Dan. Right. So she named her car after me, her classic car. Now, by the way, I got a mountain of this kind of stuff. People send me pictures of their houses. Sure. And so those are really great pictorial testimonials. Right. So and now if you were doing this and you got that, you would then want to go capture video. Sure. You would want to have her tell you the story while you were with the car or in the car or you know, you know, whatever. So where do you use these? I mean, where are the different places that you can use them everywhere? Okay, everywhere you can. And so most people don't use them enough and they don't use them in as many places as they could or should. But so the number one car salesman, Ford salesman for years in the Southwest is the guy who was my car. I got cars from when I lived in Phoenix and when I was young. His all three walls, because to the back to you is open, Florida ceiling were snapshots, the old Polaroid camera that you got the instant picture out of, snapshots of happy smiling people standing next to their cars with their names written on them. And there were visual patterns based on referrals. So you saw mine and you saw all three cars I bought from him in order. Then right underneath me, you saw wife, right, two cars. You saw my dad and mom and their car. You saw my brother and car. Now you got it and he left everybody there while he went and fetched you a Coke or a coffee. So you had time to look at it and grasp. Right. So you got two messages. Oh, and if they were friends, because that too. So then it would be written, you know, Pete Lullo friend of Dan Kennedy on the bottom of the picture. So you got two messages, both profound. One, you got this guy treats everybody right, or they wouldn't bring everybody. Therefore, it's unseemly of me to fight with him over price. That's message number one. So not only was he the number one salesperson in volume, he was the number one salesperson in profit margin. Right. The second message you got is here's what you do around here. Right. Here's what you do is you refer. That's what happens here. Right. So they impose discipline upon you in order to show up consistently and frequently. And so they are foundational. They are a great place to showcase clients and testimonials and to recognize people who refer. So it gives you a media in which to dispense recognition, thereby reinforcing to everybody. The third thing it does is it provides an internal lead generation media for you to generate leads for specific product services, promotions, and offers. And as you can see, they follow a very specific format. They're not difficult to use at all. So, Dan, one other thing about the newsletters is I noticed that there's a lot of entertainment factor in all of these newsletters here. Well, so what most people do if they go do this on their own, or a lot of companies that sell the service in different niches, because there's usually at least two or three vendors in real estate, in insurance and so forth, who are providing standardized, we do it, we print it, we mail it. What you will find most of them doing is core deliverable centric newsletters. People do it because they're fascinated with plumbing, and therefore they think everybody else is fascinated with plumbing. I like my plumber when I need them, but I'm really not all that into the pipe of the month kind of conversation. But people do it all the time. So, if you think about what does a public read, less and less, what does a public read and what do they read most? Entertainment, natural fire. They read entertainment, pop culture, gossip. So, they read for pleasure far more than they read for information. The fiction category is much bigger than the non-fiction category. Go to Barnes and Noble Store and walk around, and count the shelf space. And that will tell you what the bulk of your newsletter ought to be. So, it ought to be about people, places, things. It ought to be entertainment, crossword puzzles, game of the month, recipes. Still the number one selling category of all books is cookbooks. A few are for people to cook, but... They buy the books. They buy the books. They may be hopeful gifts, I'm not sure, but it's a big category. So, really good newsletters are sort of readers digest people magazine, pop culture reference. So, helpful hints, but not necessarily narrowed to how to keep your muffler from falling off. And that has to be limited. It's only part because you need readership to win. Somebody has to want it. Somebody has to... Oh, I'll see what's... I can't wait to see what Dave has in here this month. And the mistake is making it all technical and tied to the core deliverable. And again, in the bonus CD and transcript that you got with this program, it goes through and Sean really explains it. And then there's plenty of examples of newsletters, client newsletters that he's produced for his clients that are in there. So, you can see it and check it out. So, let's move on to the stealth way. A stealth way to get clients to refer even when they don't want to. So, what is this stealth way and how does it work? Well, don't want to might be a bit strong, but feel uncomfortable or awkward doing it. All right. So, the chiropractor as a patient... So, first of all, the patient came to the chiropractor because of back pain from an auto injury. So, the frame of reference, the way Betty, the patient thinks of Dave, the doctor, is has somebody who fixes backs after auto injuries. So, her natural referral activity is going to be limited to a friend, family member, a co-worker who has a car wreck and is then complaining about back pain. So, that's like our problem number one. Problem number two is it's still somewhat... It's more aggressive for most than most people are really organically comfortable. Hey, Dave, you're complaining about back pain all the time. You should really go see my chiropractor. Let me tell you about my chiropractor, etc., etc. So, the... Well, that improves with what we talked about in session two. Right. Yeah. Right. With the shock of the WAPAC. But what gives them a better opportunity, a better way of doing things, is to be referring to information rather than referring to a provider. Oh, so referring to the information as opposed to directly referring to you. Yeah. So, we tell people about books that we've read all the time. We tell people about TV shows we watch all the time. We tell people about a website they can go to all the time. Right. Much more natural behavior for us. So, we use a system that I call the special report of the month system, which is every month to have a different condition problem, need, desire, specific report, that is purely informational. So, now the chiropractor, one month, might be all about headaches. So, the first thing we've done is Betty, who was only thinking about you for car wrecks and back pain, now thinks about you and she has a coworker who always is complaining about headaches and has to take a day off every so often because of migraines and so forth. Then we've given Betty a much easier way to make that connection because she can now go to a coworker and say, there's this really cool free report, 42 things you can do to eliminate headaches. Yeah. And here's where you can get it. I thought it would be useful. I thought you'd want to know about it. Oh, that's great. And so, the referral to information is far more natural for people than the referral to the provider. It's also, which we talked about in the first segment, it is much more likely, which is an important part of the criteria of why people refer or don't refer. The person referring now, Betty, in our scenario, is much more likely to get warm and enthusiastic gratitude for being told about this information than being pitched on going to the chiropractor. So, the special report of the month allows you to substitute information to refer to rather than a person, a place, a business to refer to. Yeah. So, it's a two-step process because it's like lead generation. Well, that's exactly what it is. Yeah. Because what you're doing, you're really asking in the other scenario, which you should be doing, but the other scenario, you're asking your customer, client, or patient to make a sale almost, right? Or get very close to making a sale. This one, you're just saying... And they're like leading yet. Yeah, that's beautiful. So, you know, when you go to a store, all the dumb stores run by dumb people with dumb clerks in them, have the clerk come up to you and say, can I help you? And 90% of the people say no, even though they might need help and they might want help. They say, no, I'm just looking. I just did it myself the other day. I mean, it's like... And afterwards, I'm thinking, wait a minute, I just need some help. So, I'm in this toy store, which I went to go get pirate stuff for the wealth academy. So, I walk in and the guy owns a little toy store says, can I help you? I said, no, I'm just going to browse. Now, I'm there for a specific purpose. The one reason I'm there is to find some pirate stuff. Why don't I say, got any pirate stuff and shortcut this process? But we like do it automatically. So, somebody starts to pitch us. So, Betty starts to tell you about her chiropractor because of your headaches. Your reaction often is, no, no, no. It's not that bad. It's not that bad. I went to a chiropractor 13 years ago and I don't like it. Oh, I got a doctor. Oh, I... So, there's all this instant rebuffing. And that only happened. Only has to happen to Betty once or twice. And here's where to go get it. Right. So, as soon as we switch from referral to provider, to referral to information, we will get more referrals because we just made it easier, more natural, less awkward for the person to refer. Next, if we do it report of the month, promotion of the month, we have the benefit of specificity. So, another reason why referrals don't happen is because it's all too general. Right. Right. Right. Right. Right. So, gee, it would be really great, Dave, if you told everybody about me and my restaurant. Great. But one month of promotion is all about wedding season and catering. And the next month, it's all about a free report on, you know. Valentine's Day. Yeah. Or how to do your summer picnics and classroom unions and stuff without everybody getting food poisoning and whatever. So, we have created 12 specific things for me to tell Charlie about. And I may not tell him about all 12 of them all year, but one of them maybe is going to be really of interest. Right. So, if I'm the Cairo, and this month is all about how to hit the golf ball harder and straighter than you've ever hit before, right, which comes from having great flexibility and better car parking adjustments. If I golf with you, it's much better for me to, instead of trying to convince you to go see my chiropractor to refer you to my chiropractor, all I got to do is tell you about this great report about how to hit, you want to stop that slice? There's this report you can go get. Really? Right. I'll go get it. Right. Yeah. You know, I was just thinking, this is perfect for the internet guys. I mean, it's the ideal scenario. It's ideal. And in fact, if you think about it, one of our, one of our very good members, Rich Sheffrin, built his entire business around the internet manifesto. And that was all done by referral. So, hey, you got to go get this guy's report. Hey, you got to go get this. Now you got to hire this guy. Now that you got to join his program, see exact same thing. And it went viral. So, online, this is a very extremely powerful strategy because it can actually go crazy viral. And you can put them on the shelf and dust them off 8, 10, 12 months later and use them again. It's not like you got to do a new one every month for the rest of your life. There's not a lot of difference, by the way, between actually the guts of these two reports, the title, the cover, the opening and ending salvo is different. Dan, let's go to some questions here that the folks have here. The first question comes from Shirley Voss. How do you actually go about asking anything like that? So we've got another question. Here this comes from Jeffrey Wolfe. With affluent clients, it seems exclusivity actually increases with your master of the stand, with actually increases with properly implemented referral systems. Are there any key differences when dealing with the affluent, seeking referral from the affluent? His question has its answer. The only difference is that it's more productive and more necessary because the higher up the ladder of affluence you go, the more a person's first action upon need or desire is to call a trusted friend or peer and ask for a referral. Okay. So if you work at a convenience store and you're driving to work this morning, your car's making a funny noise. You may just, when you leave work, stop at whatever place is close. If you've got your mobile phone with you while you're supposed to be working, you might Google auto repair. You might look through the, oh, here's a coupon. There's all sorts of ways you might kind of almost indiscriminately pick to get your problem with the car fixed. You're now driving to your office in your Bentley. And it's making a funny noise. If you don't already have somebody to take care of it, there's somebody in your Rolodex you call and you say, who takes care of your car? So the first impulse of an affluent person is friend, relative, neighbor, peer who would know. So Donald Trump's hairstylist drops dead and he needs a hairstylist. He's not using a coupon out of Valpac. It's not happening. He's calling somebody and saying, who do you go to? All of a sudden, my person ran off to Bermuda with her boyfriend. Where do you go? So this makes it more necessary to do this well. But it also makes it infinitely more productive to do it well. And furthermore, toward the top end, now not mass affluent, not the Neiman Marcus shopper versus the Kohl's shopper, but levels above that. They form real clans, kind of gated communities, not necessarily physically, but and what happens is if you penetrate it with one, they have this huge formidable defense, but once you're inside it, they have no defenses at all. They are 100 percent susceptible to referral. That's why a thing like Bernie Madoff happens again and again, although Bernie did it more magnificently at the high end than just about anybody else. But it's why it happens and why it's basically impossible to stop. Because once he penetrates the defenses of the small clan of, let's say, super affluent CEOs of university endowment funds, of which there's maybe, I don't know, 500, a thousand in the car, if that many. Once he's in with one, he is accepted, embraced, and unquestioned by all the rest of them because they know they have this formidable defense they've built. And so he now runs amok, all on referrals. And he doesn't have to get them directly, all he's got to do is be seen with the one he out. Right. Right. Wow. Powerful. All right. Doug Keady says we saw.
